name: Brahmananda Sarasvati (Shankaracharya of Jyotirmath)

dates_evidence.description: Jyotirmath Maṭha records and modern published biographies.

core_thesis: Brahmānanda Sarasvatī (1871–1953), Śaṅkarācārya of Jyotirmath from 1941 to 1953, is the modern revivalist face of orthodox Advaita Vedānta within the Daśanāmī monastic tradition. His project is the practical re-establishment of the Jyotirmath seat (long vacant) and the oral transmission of Śaṅkara's *siddhānta* in the vernacular registers of mid-twentieth-century North India. Doctrinally he is conservative Vivaraṇa-line Advaita: *Brahman* alone is real, the world is *vyāvahārika*, the *jīva* is non-different from *Brahman* though appearing as *cidābhāsa* under *avidyā*, and *brahma-jñāna* — prepared by *karma* and *upāsanā*, ripened by *śravaṇa-manana-nididhyāsana* — is the unique means of *mokṣa*. His distinctive contribution is institutional and pedagogical rather than scholastic: the consolidation of a modern Jyotirmath disciple-line and the translation of high *siddhānta* into accessible Hindi *satsaṅga* discourse. Almost no formal Sanskrit *prakaraṇas* survive; the corpus is principally his recorded discourses, transmitted by disciples.

engaged_works.title: Recorded discourses
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Traditionally ascribed; few formal treatises survive — corpus consists chiefly of recorded discourses.
engaged_works.summary: Collected Hindi *satsaṅga* discourses delivered by Brahmānanda Sarasvatī during his tenure (1941–1953) as Śaṅkarācārya of Jyotirmath, recorded by disciples and circulated in modern editions. Doctrinally the discourses present orthodox Vivaraṇa-line Advaita in the vernacular pedagogical register: the unreality of the world as *vyāvahārika*, the analytic non-difference of *jīva* and *Brahman*, the role of *karma*, *upāsanā*, *bhakti*, and *jñāna* in the path to *mokṣa*, the discipline of *śravaṇa-manana-nididhyāsana*, and the necessity of a qualified *guru*. They matter primarily as a witness to twentieth-century Daśanāmī Advaita pedagogy and to the transmission of orthodox *siddhānta* outside the technical Sanskrit *śāstra*-idiom; the corpus is foundational for several modern lineages claiming descent from the Jyotirmath seat. (NB: this is the modern Jyotirmath Śaṅkarācārya, distinct from the seventeenth-century Gauḍa-Brahmānanda Sarasvatī who authored the *Laghu-Candrikā* — flagged as skeleton mapping mismatch with prompt.)
